sections: History of the People's Republic of China
Jiang Zemin and the third generation (1989–2002)
Transition of power and Deng's Southern Tour
Hu Jintao and the fourth generation (2002–2012)
Xi Jinping and the fifth generation (2012–present)
first_paragraph: On 1 October 1949 [CCP chairman](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party") [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") [proclaimed](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China") the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") (PRC) from atop [Tiananmen](/wiki/Tiananmen "Tiananmen"), after a [near complete victory](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution") (1949) by the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP) in the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War"). The PRC is the most recent political entity to govern [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), preceded by the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") (ROC; 1912–1949) and thousands of years of [monarchical](/wiki/Monarchy_of_China "Monarchy of China") [dynasties](/wiki/Dynasties_in_Chinese_history "Dynasties in Chinese history"). The [paramount leaders](/wiki/Paramount_leader "Paramount leader") have been Mao Zedong (1949–1976); [Hua Guofeng](/wiki/Hua_Guofeng "Hua Guofeng") (1976–1978); [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") (1978–1989); [Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin") (1989–2002); [Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao") (2002–2012); and [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") (2012 to present).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

On 1 October 1949 [CCP chairman](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party") [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") [proclaimed](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China") the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") (PRC) from atop [Tiananmen](/wiki/Tiananmen "Tiananmen"), after a [near complete victory](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution") (1949) by the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP) in the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War"). The PRC is the most recent political entity to govern [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), preceded by the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") (ROC; 1912–1949) and thousands of years of [monarchical](/wiki/Monarchy_of_China "Monarchy of China") [dynasties](/wiki/Dynasties_in_Chinese_history "Dynasties in Chinese history"). The [paramount leaders](/wiki/Paramount_leader "Paramount leader") have been Mao Zedong (1949–1976); [Hua Guofeng](/wiki/Hua_Guofeng "Hua Guofeng") (1976–1978); [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") (1978–1989); [Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin") (1989–2002); [Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao") (2002–2012); and [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") (2012 to present).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The origins of the People's Republic can be traced to the [Chinese Soviet Republic](/wiki/Chinese_Soviet_Republic "Chinese Soviet Republic") proclaimed in 1931, with the backing of the [Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union"), which included the [Jiangxi](/wiki/Jiangxi_Soviet "Jiangxi Soviet") and [Yan'an Soviets](/wiki/Yan%27an_Soviet "Yan'an Soviet").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) in the midst of the Chinese Civil War against the [Nationalist government](/wiki/Nationalist_government "Nationalist government") only to dissolve in 1937.[[4]](#cite_note-4) By 1949, the CCP took control of most of mainland China, and the [ROC government retreated offshore to Taiwan](/wiki/Retreat_of_the_government_of_the_Republic_of_China_to_Taiwan "Retreat of the government of the Republic of China to Taiwan").[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1950, the PRC [captured Hainan](/wiki/Battle_of_Hainan_Island "Battle of Hainan Island") from the ROC[[6]](#cite_note-6) and [annexed Tibet](/wiki/Annexation_of_Tibet_by_China "Annexation of Tibet by China").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The CCP consolidated its popularity among the peasants through the [Land Reform Movement](/wiki/Land_Reform_Movement "Land Reform Movement"), which included the state-tolerated executions of between 1 and 2 million landlords by peasants and former tenants.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Though the PRC initially allied closely with the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), the relations between the two [communist](/wiki/Communism "Communism") nations [gradually deteriorated](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split "Sino-Soviet split"), leading China to develop an independent industrial system and [its own nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_of_China "Nuclear weapons of China").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The Chinese population increased from 550 million in 1950 to 900 million in 1974.[[10]](#cite_note-10) However, the [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward"), a massive [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") project, resulted in [an estimated 15 to 55 million deaths](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine") between 1959 and 1961, mostly from starvation.[[11]](#cite_note-nyt-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 1964, China [detonated its first atomic bomb](/wiki/Project_596 "Project 596").[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 1966, Mao and his allies launched the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"), sparking a decade of political recrimination and social upheaval that lasted until Mao's [death](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Mao_Zedong "Death and state funeral of Mao Zedong") in 1976.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Ud%C3%A1losti_na_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Tian_an_men%2C_%C4%8C%C3%ADna_1989%2C_foto_Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Tondl.jpg/250px-Ud%C3%A1losti_na_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Tian_an_men%2C_%C4%8C%C3%ADna_1989%2C_foto_Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Tondl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ud%C3%A1losti_na_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Tian_an_men,_%C4%8C%C3%ADna_1989,_foto_Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Tondl.jpg)

The [1989 Tiananmen Square protests](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests "1989 Tiananmen Square protests").

After Mao's death, the [Gang of Four](/wiki/Gang_of_Four "Gang of Four") were [arrested](/wiki/Smashing_the_Gang_of_Four "Smashing the Gang of Four") by [Hua Guofeng](/wiki/Hua_Guofeng "Hua Guofeng") and held responsible for the Cultural Revolution. The Cultural Revolution was rebuked, with millions rehabilitated. [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") took power in 1978, and started the [reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up"), instituting large-scale [political](/wiki/Boluan_Fanzheng "Boluan Fanzheng") and economic reforms, together with the "[Eight Elders](/wiki/Eight_Elders "Eight Elders")", most senior and influential members of the party. The economic reforms marked China's transition away from planned economy.[[15]](#cite_note-Ref_e-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Hamrin-1995-16) China adopted its current [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_China "Constitution of China") on 4 December 1982.[[17]](#cite_note-17) In 1989, there were protests such [those in Tiananmen Square](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests_and_massacre "1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre"), and then throughout the entire nation.[[18]](#cite_note-18) [Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin") was elevated to become the CCP general secretary, becoming the paramount leader.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) China's economy grew sevenfold during this time.[[21]](#cite_note-APs-2022-21) [British Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong") and [Portuguese Macau](/wiki/Portuguese_Macau "Portuguese Macau") returned to China in [1997](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong") and [1999](/wiki/Handover_of_Macau "Handover of Macau"), respectively, as [special administrative regions](/wiki/Special_administrative_regions_of_China "Special administrative regions of China") under the principle of [one country, two systems](/wiki/One_country,_two_systems "One country, two systems"). The country joined the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") in 2001.[[21]](#cite_note-APs-2022-21)

In 2002, [Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao") succeeded Jiang as the general secretary.[[21]](#cite_note-APs-2022-21) Under Hu, China maintained its high rate of economic growth, overtaking the United Kingdom, France, Germany and Japan to become the world's second-largest economy.[[22]](#cite_note-22) However, the growth also severely impacted the country's resources and environment,[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) and caused major social displacement.[[25]](#cite_note-Ref_k-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") succeeded as paramount leader in 2012. Shortly after his ascension to power, Xi launched [a vast anti-corruption crackdown](/wiki/Anti-corruption_campaign_under_Xi_Jinping "Anti-corruption campaign under Xi Jinping"),[[27]](#cite_note-27) that prosecuted more than 2 million officials by 2022.[[28]](#cite_note-Marquis-2022b-28) During [his tenure](/wiki/China_under_Xi_Jinping "China under Xi Jinping"), Xi has consolidated power unseen since the initiation of economic and political reforms.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In the 21st century, its new wealth and technology led to a contest for primacy in Asian affairs versus [India](/wiki/China%E2%80%93India_relations "China–India relations"), [Japan](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Japan_relations "China–Japan relations") and the [United States](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_relations "China–United States relations"), and since 2018 a growing [trade war with the United States](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_trade_war "China–United States trade war").[[30]](#cite_note-Jonathan_Fenby_2019-30)

## Mao era (1949–1976)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Mao era (1949–1976)")]

See also: [History of the People's Republic of China (1949–1976)](/wiki/History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China_(1949%E2%80%931976) "History of the People's Republic of China (1949–1976)")

### Socialist transformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Socialist transformation")]

Main articles: [Proclamation of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China"), [Chinese Land Reform](/wiki/Chinese_Land_Reform "Chinese Land Reform"), and [Anti-Rightist Campaign](/wiki/Anti-Rightist_Campaign "Anti-Rightist Campaign")

Following the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") and [victory](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution") of Mao Zedong's [Communist](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") forces over the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") forces of Generalissimo [Chiang Kai-shek](/wiki/Chiang_Kai-shek "Chiang Kai-shek"), who fled to [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), Mao Zedong [proclaimed](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China") the founding of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") (PRC) on 1 October 1949. Mao laid heavy theoretical emphasis on [command economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy") and [class struggle](/wiki/Class_conflict "Class conflict"). [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") ended in 1953, Mao Zedong launched campaigns to persecute former landlords and merchants, starting the [industrialisation program](/wiki/Industry_of_China "Industry of China") at the same time. Mao's first goal was a total overhaul of the land ownership system, and extensive land reforms, including the execution of more powerful landlords. China's old system of [gentry](/wiki/Gentry "Gentry") [landlord](/wiki/Landlord "Landlord") ownership of farmland and tenant peasants was replaced with a distribution system in favor of poor/landless peasants which significantly reduced [economic inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality"). Over a million landlords were executed in the [Chinese land reform](/wiki/Chinese_Land_Reform "Chinese Land Reform").[[31]](#cite_note-31) In Zhangzhuangcun, in the more thoroughly reformed north of the country, most "landlords" and "rich peasants" had lost all their land and often their lives or had fled. The campaign resulted in hundreds of millions of peasants receiving a plot of land for the first time.[[32]](#cite_note-Scheidel226-32) As a result, "middling peasants", who now accounted for 90% of the village population, owned 91% of the land.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Drug trafficking and opium use were largely wiped out. Foreign investments were seized and outsiders were expelled. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At the same time, political movements and class struggles were launched nationwide. The [Anti-Rightist Campaign](/wiki/Anti-Rightist_Campaign "Anti-Rightist Campaign") of 1957–1958 significantly damaged the democracy in China, during which at least 550,000 people were persecuted, most of whom were intellectuals and political dissidents.[[34]](#cite_note-34) After the campaign, China entered the *de facto* [one-party state](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state") of the Chinese Communist Party. Other major political movements in 1950s included the [Suppression of Counter-revolutionaries](/wiki/Campaign_to_Suppress_Counterrevolutionaries "Campaign to Suppress Counterrevolutionaries"), the [Three-anti and Five-anti Campaigns](/wiki/Three-anti_and_Five-anti_Campaigns "Three-anti and Five-anti Campaigns") and the [Sufan Movement](/wiki/Sufan_movement "Sufan movement"), each of which resulted in a large number of deaths nationwide. Kuomingtang officials and soldiers who remained in mainland China were purged during the Counterrevolutionaries Suppression Campaign (鎮壓反革命運動; 1950-1953), where reported over 1 million were executed. It was followed by the Three-Anti & Five-Anti Campaigns (三反五反運動;1951-1952), during which corrupt CCP officials and other "enemies of the people", mostly in cities, were purged or even summarily executed. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Great Leap Forward and aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Great Leap Forward and aftermath")]

Main articles: [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward") and [Great Chinese Famine](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Struggles_in_front_of_the_open_hearth_furnace.jpg/250px-Struggles_in_front_of_the_open_hearth_furnace.jpg)](/wiki/File:Struggles_in_front_of_the_open_hearth_furnace.jpg)

People were required to produce more steel during the Great Leap Forward.

Mao Zedong believed that [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") would eventually triumph over all other ideologies, and following the [First Five-Year Plan](/wiki/First_five-year_plan_(China) "First five-year plan (China)") based on a Soviet-style centrally controlled economy, Mao took on the ambitious project of the [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward") in 1958, beginning an unprecedented process of [collectivisation](/wiki/Collective_farming "Collective farming") in rural areas (the [People's commune](/wiki/People%27s_commune "People's commune")). Mao urged the use of communally organised iron smelters to increase steel production, pulling workers off of agricultural labor to the point that large amounts of crops rotted unharvested. Mao decided to continue to advocate these smelters despite a visit to a factory steel mill which proved to him that high quality steel could only be produced in a factory. He thought that ending the program would dampen peasant enthusiasm for his political mobilisation, the Great Leap Forward. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The implementation of [Maoism](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism") thought in China may have been responsible for the [deadliest famine in human history](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine"), in which 15–55 million people died due to starvation and epidemics.[[35]](#cite_note-Fenby-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Meng-2015-36)[[37]](#cite_note-Wemheuer-2011-37) By the end of 1961, the birth rate was nearly cut in half because of malnutrition.[[38]](#cite_note-38) In 1958, the [Xunhua uprising](/wiki/Xunhua_Incident "Xunhua Incident") broke out and in 1959, [a major uprising](/wiki/1959_Tibetan_uprising "1959 Tibetan uprising") erupted in [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet"), resulting in the deaths of tens of thousands of Tibetans, and the [Dalai Lama](/wiki/Dalai_Lama "Dalai Lama") went into exile afterwards.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) Meeting criticisms from [Marshal](/wiki/Yuanshuai "Yuanshuai") [Peng Dehuai](/wiki/Peng_Dehuai "Peng Dehuai") and others at the [Lushan conference](/wiki/Lushan_Conference "Lushan Conference") over the radical policies, Mao launched the massive "[Anti-Right Deviation Struggle](/wiki/Anti-Right_Deviation_Struggle "Anti-Right Deviation Struggle")" in 1959,[[41]](#cite_note-:03-41) during which over 3 million CCP members were labelled as "right-deviationists" or "right-opportunists" and were subsequently purged or penalized.[[42]](#cite_note-:3-42) However, Mao's failure with the Leap reduced his power in government, whose administrative duties fell to President [Liu Shaoqi](/wiki/Liu_Shaoqi "Liu Shaoqi") and [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping"), especially after the [Seven Thousand Cadres Conference](/wiki/Seven_Thousand_Cadres_Conference "Seven Thousand Cadres Conference") in early 1962. The power struggle between Mao Zedong and Liu Shaoqi together with Deng Xiaoping began after 1962. The [Socialist Education Movement](/wiki/Socialist_Education_Movement "Socialist Education Movement") was launched by Mao from 1963 to 1965, as a result. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Much more successful was the "[Two Bombs, One Satellite](/wiki/Two_Bombs,_One_Satellite "Two Bombs, One Satellite")" program, launched in 1958, with the help at first of Moscow.[[43]](#cite_note-43) It used leading scientists who returned to mainland China from abroad, including [Qian Xuesen](/wiki/Qian_Xuesen "Qian Xuesen"), [Deng Jiaxian](/wiki/Deng_Jiaxian "Deng Jiaxian") and [Qian Sanqiang](/wiki/Qian_Sanqiang "Qian Sanqiang").[[44]](#cite_note-44) China's first [atomic bomb](/wiki/Atomic_Bomb "Atomic Bomb"), [nuclear missile](/wiki/Dongfeng_(missile) "Dongfeng (missile)"), [hydrogen bomb](/wiki/Hydrogen_bomb "Hydrogen bomb") and [artificial satellite](/wiki/Artificial_satellite "Artificial satellite") were all successfully developed by 1970. However, the program had been seriously affected by the Great Leap Forward and the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"). [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Third Front

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Third Front")]

Main article: [Third Front (China)](/wiki/Third_Front_(China) "Third Front (China)")

After the failure of the Great Leap Forward, China's leadership slowed the pace of industrialization.[[45]](#cite_note-:02-45): 3 It invested more on in China's coastal regions and focused on the production of consumer goods.[[45]](#cite_note-:02-45): 3 After an April 1964 General Staff report concluded that the concentration of China's industry in its major coastal cities made it vulnerable to attack by foreign powers, Mao argued for the development of basic industry and national defense industry in protected locations in China's interior.[[45]](#cite_note-:02-45): 4, 54 This resulted in the building of the Third Front, which involved massive projects including railroad infrastructure,[[45]](#cite_note-:02-45): 153–164 aerospace industry including satellite launch facilities,[[45]](#cite_note-:02-45): 218–219 and steel production industry including [Panzhihua Iron and Steel](/wiki/Panzhihua_Iron_and_Steel "Panzhihua Iron and Steel").[[45]](#cite_note-:02-45): 9

Development of the Third Front slowed in 1966 during the Cultural Revolution, but accelerated again after the [Sino-Soviet border conflict](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_border_conflict "Sino-Soviet border conflict") at Zhenbao Island, which increased the perceived risk of Soviet Invasion.[[45]](#cite_note-:02-45): 12, 150 Third Front construction again decreased after United States President [Richard Nixon's 1972 visit to China](/wiki/1972_visit_by_Richard_Nixon_to_China "1972 visit by Richard Nixon to China") and the resulting rapprochement between the United States and China.[[45]](#cite_note-:02-45): 225–229 When Reform and Opening up began after Mao's death, China began to gradually wind down Third Front projects.[[46]](#cite_note-:92-46): 180 The Third Front distributed physical and human capital around the country, ultimately decreased regional disparities and created favorable conditions for later market development.[[46]](#cite_note-:92-46): 177–182

### Cultural Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Cultural Revolution")]

Main article: [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/1967-02_1967%E5%B9%B4%E7%9A%84%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5.jpg/250px-1967-02_1967%E5%B9%B4%E7%9A%84%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5.jpg)](/wiki/File:1967-02_1967%E5%B9%B4%E7%9A%84%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5.jpg)

[Red Guards](/wiki/Red_Guards "Red Guards") on [Tiananmen Square](/wiki/Tiananmen_Square "Tiananmen Square") during the Cultural Revolution (1967)

In 1963, Mao Zedong launched the Socialist Education Movement, which is regarded as the precursor of the Cultural Revolution. To impose socialist orthodoxy and rid China of "[Four Olds](/wiki/Four_Olds "Four Olds")", and at the same time serving certain political goals, Mao began the Cultural Revolution in May 1966, attempting to return to the center of political power in China. The campaign was far reaching into all aspects of Chinese life. Estimated death toll ranges from millions to 20,000,000.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-Pye-1986-48)[[49]](#cite_note-WangS-2012-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Song-2011c-50) [Massacres](/wiki/List_of_massacres_in_China#1966–1976_(Cultural_Revolution) "List of massacres in China") took place across the country while [massive cannibalism](/wiki/Guangxi_Massacre "Guangxi Massacre") also occurred.[[51]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-51)[[52]](#cite_note-:05-52) Starting from the "[Red August](/wiki/Red_August "Red August")" of 1966 in Beijing, [Red Guards](/wiki/Red_Guards "Red Guards") terrorized the streets as many ordinary citizens were deemed counter-revolutionaries; education and public transportation came to a nearly complete halt.[[53]](#cite_note-Wang-2001-53)

In 1967, [rebel groups](/wiki/Rebel_Faction_(Cultural_Revolution) "Rebel Faction (Cultural Revolution)") began [seizing power](/wiki/Seizure_of_power_(Cultural_Revolution) "Seizure of power (Cultural Revolution)") from local governments and party branches, establishing new [revolutionary committees](/wiki/Revolutionary_committee_(China) "Revolutionary committee (China)") in their place meanwhile [smashing the police, procuratorate and judicial systems](/wiki/Smashing_gong-jian-fa "Smashing gong-jian-fa"). These committees often split into rival factions, engaging in the deadly [violent struggles](/wiki/Violent_Struggle "Violent Struggle").[[51]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-51) Large-scale political purges such as [Cleansing the Class Ranks](/wiki/Cleansing_the_Class_Ranks "Cleansing the Class Ranks") and [One Strike-Three Anti Campaign](/wiki/One_Strike-Three_Anti_Campaign "One Strike-Three Anti Campaign") swept across the country; many prominent political leaders, including Liu Shaoqi and Deng Xiaoping, were purged and deemed "[capitalist roaders](/wiki/Capitalist_roader "Capitalist roader")". Millions of people were labelled as members of the [Five Black Categories](/wiki/Five_Black_Categories "Five Black Categories") and were persecuted, while many intellectuals and scientists were labelled as the [Stinking Old Ninth](/wiki/Stinking_Old_Ninth "Stinking Old Ninth") and went through [struggle sessions](/wiki/Struggle_session "Struggle session").[[51]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-51)[[53]](#cite_note-Wang-2001-53) In 1971, the [Lin Biao Incident](/wiki/Lin_Biao_incident "Lin Biao incident") shocked Beijing, followed by the rise of [Gang of Four](/wiki/Gang_of_Four "Gang of Four"). The Revolution would not come to a complete end until the [death of Mao Zedong](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Mao_Zedong "Death and state funeral of Mao Zedong") and arrest of the Gang of Four in 1976. The second constitution of China, known as the "[1975 Constitution](/wiki/1975_Constitution_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "1975 Constitution of the People's Republic of China")", was passed in 1975 during the Cultural Revolution. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On the other hand, by the time of Mao's death, China's unity and [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") were assured for the first time in a century, and there was development of [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure"), [industry](/wiki/Industrial_sector "Industrial sector"), [healthcare](/wiki/Healthcare "Healthcare"), [education](/wiki/Education "Education") (only 20% of the population could read in 1949, compared to 65.5% thirty years later),[[54]](#cite_note-54) which raised [standard of living](/wiki/Standard_of_living "Standard of living") for the average Chinese. There is also an argument that [campaigns](/wiki/Political_campaign "Political campaign") such as the Great Leap Forward – an example of the concept [New Democracy](/wiki/New_Democracy "New Democracy") – and the Cultural Revolution were essential in jumpstarting China's development and "purifying" its culture: even though the consequences of both these campaigns were economically and humanly disastrous, they left behind a "clean slate" on which later economic progress could be built.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

Before the [Sino-Soviet split](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split "Sino-Soviet split") in the 1960s, the primary foreign policy of the People's Republic of China was to obtain diplomatic recognition in the face of strong American opposition.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The [Bandung Conference](/wiki/Bandung_Conference "Bandung Conference") in 1955, at which Premier [Zhou Enlai](/wiki/Zhou_Enlai "Zhou Enlai") led the Chinese delegation, was an important milestone for China's foreign relations.[[57]](#cite_note-:322-57): 80 China developed its foreign relations with many newly independent and soon-to-be independent countries.[[57]](#cite_note-:322-57): 80 China's [Five Principles of Peaceful Coexistence](/wiki/Five_Principles_of_Peaceful_Coexistence "Five Principles of Peaceful Coexistence") were incorporated into the Ten Principles of Bandung.[[57]](#cite_note-:322-57): 80In 1964, tensions between Washington and Paris [allowed France](/wiki/China%E2%80%93France_relations "China–France relations") to open relations.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

There were also sporadic military confrontations between the People's Republic of China and the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China") based in Taiwan. Around the [Dongshan Island](/wiki/Dongshan_Island "Dongshan Island") in [Fujian Province](/wiki/Fujian_Province "Fujian Province"), for example, there were [Battle of Dongshan Island](/wiki/Battle_of_Dongshan_Island "Battle of Dongshan Island") in 1950 and the [Dongshan Island Campaign](/wiki/Dongshan_Island_Campaign "Dongshan Island Campaign") in 1953 between the [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") and the [Republic of China Armed Forces](/wiki/Republic_of_China_Armed_Forces "Republic of China Armed Forces"), followed by another [Battle of Dongshan](/wiki/Battle_of_Dongshan "Battle of Dongshan") in August 1965.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

#### Soviet Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Soviet Union")]

Main articles: [Sino-Soviet relations](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_relations "Sino-Soviet relations") and [Sino-Soviet split](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split "Sino-Soviet split")

Beijing was very pleased that the success of the Soviet Union in the [space race](/wiki/Space_Race "Space Race") – the original [Sputniks](/wiki/List_of_spacecraft_called_Sputnik "List of spacecraft called Sputnik") – demonstrated that the international communist movement had caught up in high technology with the Americans. Mao assumed that the Soviets now had a military advantage and should step up the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"); Khrushchev knew that the Americans were well ahead in military uses of space.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The strains multiplied, quickly making a dead letter of the 1950 alliance, destroying the socialist camp unity, and affected the world balance of power. The split started with [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") [De-Stalinization](/wiki/De-Stalinization "De-Stalinization") program. It angered Mao, who admired Stalin.[[62]](#cite_note-62) In addition, the radical policies of the Great Leap Forward as well as [Mao's controversial remarks on nuclear wars](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split#Mao's_nuclear-war_remarks_and_two_Chinas "Sino-Soviet split") troubled Moscow.[[63]](#cite_note-:19-63) According to Chinese records, the Soviet Union suddenly withdrew 1390 technicians and ended 600 contracts with China in 1960.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Moscow and Beijing became worldwide rivals, forcing communist parties around the world to take sides; many of them split, so that the pro-Soviet communists were battling the pro-Chinese communists for local control of the left-wing forces in much of the world.[[65]](#cite_note-65) At the [1960 International Meeting of Communist and Workers Parties](/wiki/1960_International_Meeting_of_Communist_and_Workers_Parties "1960 International Meeting of Communist and Workers Parties"), and later at the [22nd Congress of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/22nd_Congress_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "22nd Congress of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union") in 1961, the Sino-Soviet conflict became out of control and was increasingly fought out in 81 communist parties around the world. Khrushchev personally attacked Mao as an ultra leftist – a left revisionist – and compared him to Stalin for dangerous egotism.[[66]](#cite_note-66) China later ridiculed the Russian incompetence in the [Cuban Missile Crisis](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis") of 1962 as adventurism to start with and [capitulationism](/wiki/Capitulation_(surrender) "Capitulation (surrender)") to wind up on the losing side. Moscow now was increasingly giving priority to friendly relationships and test ban treaties with the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/P2074121a500488294-ss.jpg/250px-P2074121a500488294-ss.jpg)](/wiki/File:P2074121a500488294-ss.jpg)

Mao Zedong (left) and Lin Biao (right) at the [9th National Congress of CCP](/wiki/9th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "9th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") in 1969, when Lin was named the successor of Mao.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

A crisis came in 1962, when over 60,000 refugees escaped from [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang") in [western China](/wiki/Western_China "Western China") to Soviet territory to escape persecution, historically known as the "[Yi–Ta incident](/wiki/Yi%E2%80%93Ta_incident "Yi–Ta incident")". Meanwhile, [border conflicts](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_border_conflict "Sino-Soviet border conflict") such as the [Tielieketi incident](/wiki/Tielieketi_Incident "Tielieketi Incident") quickly escalated between the two countries. The Sino-Soviet conflict culminated after the [Zhenbao Island Incident](/wiki/Zhenbao_Island_incident "Zhenbao Island incident") in 1969, when the Soviet Union led by [Leonid Brezhnev](/wiki/Leonid_Brezhnev "Leonid Brezhnev") planned to [launch a large-scale nuclear strike on China](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split#Planned_Soviet_nuclear_strike_on_China "Sino-Soviet split"), including major cities like [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Changchun](/wiki/Changchun "Changchun") and [Anshan](/wiki/Anshan "Anshan"), as well as China's nuclear sites including [Jiuquan](/wiki/Jiuquan "Jiuquan"), [Xichang](/wiki/Xichang "Xichang") and [Lop Nur](/wiki/Lop_Nur "Lop Nur").[[72]](#cite_note-:32-72)[[73]](#cite_note-:4-73)[[74]](#cite_note-:5-74)[[75]](#cite_note-:7-75) On October 14, 1969, the [Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") released an urgent evacuation order to the [Party and state leaders](/wiki/Party_and_state_leaders "Party and state leaders") in Beijing, requiring all leaders to leave Beijing by October 20, with Mao travelling to [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan "Wuhan") and [Lin Biao](/wiki/Lin_Biao "Lin Biao") travelling to [Suzhou](/wiki/Suzhou "Suzhou").[[72]](#cite_note-:32-72)[[76]](#cite_note-:13-76) Lin also issued the "[Order Number One](/wiki/Order_Number_One_(Lin_Biao) "Order Number One (Lin Biao)")" on October 18, putting all [PLA](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") personnel on alert for imminent attack from the Soviet Union.[[76]](#cite_note-:13-76)[[77]](#cite_note-:04-77) Eventually, the Soviet called off the attack due to the intervention from the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[72]](#cite_note-:32-72)[[75]](#cite_note-:7-75) Increasingly, Beijing began to consider the Soviet Union, which it viewed as [Social imperialism](/wiki/Social_imperialism "Social imperialism"), as the greatest threat it faced, more so than even the leading [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") power, the United States. In turn, overtures were made between the PRC and the United States, such as in the [Ping Pong Diplomacy](/wiki/Ping-pong_diplomacy "Ping-pong diplomacy"), [Panda Diplomacy](/wiki/Panda_diplomacy "Panda diplomacy") and the [1972 Nixon visit to China](/wiki/1972_visit_by_Richard_Nixon_to_China "1972 visit by Richard Nixon to China").[[78]](#cite_note-78)

#### Major diplomatic relations established

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Major diplomatic relations established")]

In 1950, [India](/wiki/India "India") became one of the first countries to recognize People's Republic of China and [establish formal diplomatic relation](/wiki/China%E2%80%93India_relations "China–India relations"). However, India had close ties to the USSR and in 1962, a one-month [Sino-Indian war](/wiki/Sino-Indian_war "Sino-Indian war") and also a one-month [Second Sino-Indian war](/wiki/Nathu_La_and_Cho_La_clashes "Nathu La and Cho La clashes") in 1967 broke out along their remote border. Border tensions flared from time to time ever since.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/President_Richard_Nixon_and_Mao_Zedong.jpg/250px-President_Richard_Nixon_and_Mao_Zedong.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Richard_Nixon_and_Mao_Zedong.jpg)

U.S. President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") made a [historic visit to China in 1972](/wiki/1972_visit_by_Richard_Nixon_to_China "1972 visit by Richard Nixon to China") and met with Mao Zedong.

China established formal relationships with several major [western countries](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") and Japan, primarily after the Sino-Soviet split in the 1960s. Typically, the other party broke formal relations it had with the government of [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China") (ROC) in Taiwan once it established diplomatic relations with the People's Republic of China (PRC).

* In January 1964, PRC established formal [diplomatic relations with France](/wiki/China%E2%80%93France_relations "China–France relations").[[80]](#cite_note-80)
* In October 1970, PRC established formal [diplomatic relations with Canada](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93China_relations "Canada–China relations").[[81]](#cite_note-MFA-2020-81)
* In November 1970, PRC established formal [diplomatic relations with Italy](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Italy_relations "China–Italy relations").[[81]](#cite_note-MFA-2020-81)
* In 1971, [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania")'s motion in the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") to recognize the People's Republic of China as the sole legal China (replacing the Republic of China) was passed as [General Assembly Resolution 2758](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Resolution_2758 "United Nations General Assembly Resolution 2758").
* In March 1972, China established formal [diplomatic relations with the United Kingdom](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_relations "China–United Kingdom relations").[[82]](#cite_note-82) The UK was the first major Western country to recognize the PRC in 1950.
* In September 1972, PRC established formal [diplomatic relations with Japan](/wiki/Japan%E2%80%93China_Joint_Communiqu%C3%A9 "Japan–China Joint Communiqué").[[81]](#cite_note-MFA-2020-81)
* In October 1972, PRC established formal [diplomatic relations with West Germany](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Germany_relations "China–Germany relations").[[83]](#cite_note-83)
* In December 1972, PRC established formal [diplomatic relations with Australia](/wiki/Australia%E2%80%93China_relations "Australia–China relations").[[81]](#cite_note-MFA-2020-81)
* In March 1973, PRC established formal [diplomatic relations with Spain](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Spain_relations "China–Spain relations").[[81]](#cite_note-MFA-2020-81)

| 1975 | [Haicheng earthquake](/wiki/1975_Haicheng_earthquake "1975 Haicheng earthquake") | [Liaoning](/wiki/Liaoning "Liaoning") | 1,328 | Magnitude 7.5 [Ms](/wiki/Seismic_magnitude_scales#Ms "Seismic magnitude scales").[[88]](#cite_note-88) Some claimed the death toll was 2,041. |
| 1975 | [The Banqiao Dam failure](/wiki/1975_Banqiao_Dam_failure "1975 Banqiao Dam failure") | [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan") | 85,600–240,000 | 62 dams including the largest [Banqiao Dam](/wiki/Banqiao_Dam "Banqiao Dam") in Henan province collapsed due to [Typhoon Nina of 1975](/wiki/Typhoon_Nina_(1975) "Typhoon Nina (1975)"), creating the [third-largest flood in history](/wiki/List_of_natural_disasters_by_death_toll#Ten_deadliest_floods "List of natural disasters by death toll") (according to the Chinese government, the death toll was 26,000).[[89]](#cite_note-Xu-2008-89)[[90]](#cite_note-Renmin_Wang-2012-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-Phoenix_New_Media-2008-92)[[93]](#cite_note-SJState-1975-93) It was rated No.1 in "The Ultimate 10 Technological Disasters" of the world by [Discovery Channel](/wiki/Discovery_Channel "Discovery Channel") in May 2005 ([*the Ultimate 10* show](https://web.archive.org/web/20210102060444if_/https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uDS0uSPnoQk)), beating the [Chernobyl nuclear disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster").[[90]](#cite_note-Renmin_Wang-2012-90)[[92]](#cite_note-Phoenix_New_Media-2008-92)[[94]](#cite_note-94) Most of the dams that collapsed in this disaster were built with the help of experts from [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") or during the [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward").[[92]](#cite_note-Phoenix_New_Media-2008-92)[[95]](#cite_note-internationalrivers-2020-95)[[96]](#cite_note-Renmin_Wang-1975-96)[[97]](#cite_note-ozy.com-2019-97) |
| 1976 | [Tangshan earthquake](/wiki/1976_Tangshan_earthquake "1976 Tangshan earthquake") | [Hebei](/wiki/Hebei "Hebei") | At least 242,769 | [Magnitude](/wiki/Seismic_magnitude_scales "Seismic magnitude scales") 7.6 [Mw](/wiki/Seismic_magnitude_scales#Mw "Seismic magnitude scales").[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99) |

### Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Controversies")]

See also: [List of massacres in China](/wiki/List_of_massacres_in_China "List of massacres in China")

During the Mao era, tens of millions of people died during various political movements as well as during the [Great Chinese Famine](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine"), while tens of millions of other people were persecuted and permanently crippled.[[36]](#cite_note-Meng-2015-36)[[37]](#cite_note-Wemheuer-2011-37)[[51]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-51) China turned into a *de facto* [one-party state](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state") after the [Anti-Rightist Campaign](/wiki/Anti-Rightist_Campaign "Anti-Rightist Campaign") starting in 1957, during which [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") and the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law") were damaged while at least 550,000 intellectuals and political dissidents were persecuted.[[100]](#cite_note-King-2019-100) Moreover, the Cultural Revolution severely damaged the rule of law as well as traditional Chinese culture and moral values; massacres [were committed across the country](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution#Massacres "Cultural Revolution") and acts of [cannibalism](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism") were also committed on a massive scale (e.g., [Guangxi Massacre](/wiki/Guangxi_Massacre "Guangxi Massacre")[[52]](#cite_note-:05-52)).[[51]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-51) Higher education was halted during the Cultural Revolution and scientific research was also seriously affected because many scientists were persecuted, killed or committed suicide. Some doubt statistics or accounts given for death tolls or other damages incurred by Mao's campaigns, attributing the high death toll to [natural disasters](/wiki/Natural_disaster "Natural disaster"), famine, or other consequences of political chaos during the rule of [Chiang Kai-shek](/wiki/Chiang_Kai-shek "Chiang Kai-shek").[[101]](#cite_note-101)

Mao Zedong and the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) also exported the ideology of socialism and socialist revolution to other parts of the world, especially to [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia").[[102]](#cite_note-102) Influenced and supported by Mao and the CCP, [Pol Pot](/wiki/Pol_Pot "Pol Pot") and the [Khmer Rouge](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge "Khmer Rouge") conducted the [Cambodian genocide](/wiki/Cambodian_genocide "Cambodian genocide") during which 1.5-2 million people were killed in just three years.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

## Transition and the Deng era (1976–1989)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Transition and the Deng era (1976–1989)")]

See also: [History of the People's Republic of China (1976–1989)](/wiki/History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China_(1976%E2%80%931989) "History of the People's Republic of China (1976–1989)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Deng_Xiaoping_and_Jimmy_Carter_at_the_arrival_ceremony_for_the_Vice_Premier_of_China._-_NARA_-_183157-restored%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Deng_Xiaoping_and_Jimmy_Carter_at_the_arrival_ceremony_for_the_Vice_Premier_of_China._-_NARA_-_183157-restored%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deng_Xiaoping_and_Jimmy_Carter_at_the_arrival_ceremony_for_the_Vice_Premier_of_China._-_NARA_-_183157-restored(cropped).jpg)

[Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") and [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") at the arrival ceremony for the Vice Premier of China

### The transition period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: The transition period")]

See also: [3rd plenary session of the 11th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/3rd_plenary_session_of_the_11th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "3rd plenary session of the 11th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") and [1978 Truth Criterion Controversy](/wiki/1978_Truth_Criterion_Controversy "1978 Truth Criterion Controversy")

Mao Zedong's death was followed by a power struggle between the [Gang of Four](/wiki/Gang_of_Four "Gang of Four"), [Hua Guofeng](/wiki/Hua_Guofeng "Hua Guofeng"), and eventually [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping"). The third constitution of China, known as the "[1978 Constitution](/wiki/1978_Constitution_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "1978 Constitution of the People's Republic of China")", was passed in 1978 under Hua's "[Two Whatevers](/wiki/Two_Whatevers "Two Whatevers")". [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In December 1978, with the support of [Ye Jianying](/wiki/Ye_Jianying "Ye Jianying") and other high-ranking officials, Deng eventually replaced Hua and became the [paramount leader of China](/wiki/Paramount_Leader_of_China "Paramount Leader of China") during the [3rd plenary session of the 11th Central Committee of CCP](/wiki/3rd_plenary_session_of_the_11th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "3rd plenary session of the 11th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party"). Deng's allies such as [Hu Yaobang](/wiki/Hu_Yaobang "Hu Yaobang") and [Zhao Ziyang](/wiki/Zhao_Ziyang "Zhao Ziyang") also received promotions. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Invalidating the Cultural Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Invalidating the Cultural Revolution")]

Main articles: [Boluan Fanzheng](/wiki/Boluan_Fanzheng "Boluan Fanzheng") and [New Enlightenment (China)](/wiki/New_Enlightenment_(China) "New Enlightenment (China)")

In September 1977, Deng first proposed the idea of *[Boluan Fanzheng](/wiki/Boluan_Fanzheng "Boluan Fanzheng")*, attempting to dismantle the [far-left](/wiki/Far-left_politics "Far-left politics") [Maoist policies](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism") associated with the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"). In the same year, he resumed the [National College Entrance Examination](/wiki/National_College_Entrance_Examination "National College Entrance Examination") which was cancelled for ten years due to the Cultural Revolution. Meanwhile, the "[1978 Truth Criterion Discussion](/wiki/1978_Truth_Criterion_Controversy "1978 Truth Criterion Controversy")" triggered a decade-long [New Enlightenment](/wiki/New_Enlightenment_(China) "New Enlightenment (China)") movement in mainland China, promoting [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy"), [humanism](/wiki/Humanism "Humanism") and [universal values](/wiki/Universal_value "Universal value") such as [freedom](/wiki/Freedom "Freedom"), [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") and [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law"), while opposing the ideology of Cultural Revolution.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-104)

Within several years starting 1978, victims of more than 3 million "unjust, false, wrongful cases" were rehabilitated by Deng and his allies such as [Hu Yaobang](/wiki/Hu_Yaobang "Hu Yaobang"), then [General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party").[[105]](#cite_note-Liu-2015-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106) However, on the subject of Mao's legacy, Deng coined the famous phrase "7 parts good, 3 parts bad" and avoided denouncing Mao altogether. A major document presented at the September 1979 Fourth Plenum, gave a "preliminary assessment" of the entire 30-year period of Communist rule. At the plenum, party [Vice Chairman](/wiki/Chairperson "Chairperson") [Ye Jianying](/wiki/Ye_Jianying "Ye Jianying") declared the Cultural Revolution "an appalling catastrophe" and "the most severe setback to [the] socialist cause since [1949]".[[107]](#cite_note-107)

In June 1981, the Chinese government's condemnation of the Cultural Revolution culminated in the *[Resolution on Certain Questions in the History of Our Party Since the Founding of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Resolution_on_Certain_Questions_in_the_History_of_Our_Party_since_the_Founding_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Resolution on Certain Questions in the History of Our Party since the Founding of the People's Republic of China")*, adopted by the sixth plenary session of the Eleventh Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party.[[86]](#cite_note-CPG-2008a-86)[[108]](#cite_note-Wilson_Center-1981-108) This resolution invalidated the Cultural Revolution as a "domestic havoc", but it stated that "Comrade Mao Zedong was a great Marxist and a great proletarian revolutionary, strategist and theorist. It is true that he made gross mistakes during the "Cultural Revolution", but, if we judge his activities as a whole, his contributions to the Chinese revolution far outweigh his mistakes. His merits are primary and his errors secondary".[[109]](#cite_note-109) Today, the public perception of Mao has improved at least superficially; images of Mao and Mao related objects have become fashionable, commonly used on novelty items and even as talismans. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As an aftermath of the Cultural Revolution, nationwide public safety worsened in the late 1970s and early 1980s, and as a result Deng launched the ["Strike Hard" Anti-crime Campaign](/wiki/1983_%22Strike_Hard%22_Anti-crime_Campaign "1983 \"Strike Hard\" Anti-crime Campaign") in 1983 which lasted until early 1987. More than 1.7 million people were arrested and received legal punishment during the campaign.[[110]](#cite_note-Tao-2010-110)

### Reform and opening up

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Reform and opening up")]

Main article: [Reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Light_Show_of_Shenzhen_in_May_2019_%281%29.jpg/250px-Light_Show_of_Shenzhen_in_May_2019_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Light_Show_of_Shenzhen_in_May_2019_(1).jpg)

Shenzhen, one of the first [special economic zones of China](/wiki/Special_economic_zones_of_China "Special economic zones of China") and the "Silicon Valley of China".[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112) Notable high-tech companies such as [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei"), [ZTE](/wiki/ZTE "ZTE") and [Konka](/wiki/Konka_Group "Konka Group") were all founded in [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") in the 1980s.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Xiamen_Shimao_Straits_Tower_at_dusk.jpg/250px-Xiamen_Shimao_Straits_Tower_at_dusk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xiamen_Shimao_Straits_Tower_at_dusk.jpg)

[Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"), one of the first special economic zones of China

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Zhuhai_skyline_at_night.jpg/250px-Zhuhai_skyline_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zhuhai_skyline_at_night.jpg)

[Zhuhai](/wiki/Zhuhai "Zhuhai"), one of the first special economic zones of China

At the [Third Plenum of the Eleventh National Party Congress Central Committee](/wiki/3rd_Plenary_Session_of_the_11th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "3rd Plenary Session of the 11th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party"), Deng embarked China on the road to [reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up"), policies that began with the de-collectivisation of the countryside, followed with industrial reforms aimed at decentralizing government controls in the industrial sector. In 1979, Deng emphasized the goal of "[Four Modernizations](/wiki/Four_Modernizations "Four Modernizations")" and further proposed the idea of "xiaokang", or "[moderately prosperous society](/wiki/Moderately_prosperous_society "Moderately prosperous society")".[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114) Deng laid emphasis on [light industry](/wiki/Light_industry "Light industry") as a stepping stone to the development of heavy industries. The achievements of [Lee Kuan Yew](/wiki/Lee_Kuan_Yew "Lee Kuan Yew") to create an economic superpower in [Singapore](/wiki/History_of_Singapore "History of Singapore") had a profound effect on the Communist leadership in China. Leaders in China made a major effort, especially under Deng Xiaoping, to emulate his policies of economic growth, entrepreneurship, and subtle suppression of dissent. Over the years, more than 22,000 Chinese officials were sent to Singapore to study its methods.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

Deng championed the idea of [Special Economic Zones](/wiki/Special_economic_zone "Special economic zone") (SEZ), including Shenzhen, Zhuhai and [Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"), areas where foreign investment would be allowed to pour in without strict government restraint and regulations, running on a basically [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") system.[[116]](#cite_note-116) On 31 January 1979, the [Shekou Industrial Zone](/wiki/Shekou "Shekou") of [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") was founded, becoming the first experimental area in China to "open up".[[117]](#cite_note-Heaver-2018-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118) Under the leadership of [Yuan Geng](/wiki/Yuan_Geng "Yuan Geng"), the "Shekou model" of development was gradually formed, embodied in its famous slogan "[Time is Money, Efficiency is Life](/wiki/Time_is_Money,_Efficiency_is_Life "Time is Money, Efficiency is Life")", which then widely spread to other parts of China.[[117]](#cite_note-Heaver-2018-117)[[119]](#cite_note-119) In January 1984, Deng Xiaoping made his first inspection tour to Shenzhen and Zhuhai, recognizing the "[Shenzhen Speed](/wiki/Shenzhen_speed "Shenzhen speed")" of development as well as the success of the special economics zones.[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121) With the help of Yuan Geng, the first [joint-stock](/wiki/Joint-stock_company "Joint-stock company") [commercial bank](/wiki/Commercial_bank "Commercial bank") in China – the [China Merchants Bank](/wiki/China_Merchants_Bank "China Merchants Bank") – and the first joint-stock insurance company in China – the [Ping An Insurance](/wiki/Ping_An_Insurance "Ping An Insurance") – were both established in [Shekou](/wiki/Shekou "Shekou").[[122]](#cite_note-122) In May 1984, fourteen coastal cities in China including [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") and [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin "Tianjin") were named "[Open Coastal Cities](/wiki/Special_economic_zones_of_China#List_of_the_SEZs_and_Open_Coastal_Cities "Special economic zones of China")" (沿海开放城市).[[123]](#cite_note-123)

Deng recognized the importance of [science and technology](/wiki/STEM_fields "STEM fields") in the "[Four Modernizations](/wiki/Four_Modernizations "Four Modernizations")", pointing out that "science and technology are the primary productive force".[[124]](#cite_note-124) In December 1981, he approved the construction of "[Beijing Electron–Positron Collider](/wiki/Beijing_Electron%E2%80%93Positron_Collider_II "Beijing Electron–Positron Collider II")", the first high-energy [particle collider](/wiki/Particle_collider "Particle collider") in China, and had several meetings with Nobel laureate [Tsung-Dao Lee](/wiki/Tsung-Dao_Lee "Tsung-Dao Lee") who supported the project.[[125]](#cite_note-125) In 1985, the [Great Wall Station](/wiki/Great_Wall_Station_(Antarctica) "Great Wall Station (Antarctica)"), the first Chinese [research station](/wiki/Research_station "Research station") in [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), was established. In 1986, Deng approved the proposal from four leading Chinese scientists and launched the "[863 Program](/wiki/863_Program "863 Program")"; in the same year, the nine-year [compulsory education system](/wiki/Compulsory_education "Compulsory education") was established under law (*Law on Nine-Year Compulsory Education*).[[126]](#cite_note-Tao-2020-126)[[127]](#cite_note-127) In the 1980s, [Qinshan Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Qinshan_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Qinshan Nuclear Power Plant") in [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") and [Daya Bay Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Daya_Bay_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Daya Bay Nuclear Power Plant") in Shenzhen were built, becoming the first two [nuclear power plants](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plant "Nuclear power plant") in China.[[128]](#cite_note-128) Deng also approved the appointments of foreign nationals to work in China, including the renowned [Chinese-American](/wiki/Chinese_Americans "Chinese Americans") mathematician [Shiing-Shen Chern](/wiki/Shiing-Shen_Chern "Shiing-Shen Chern").[[129]](#cite_note-129)

Supporters of the economic reforms point to the rapid development of the [consumer](/wiki/Consumer "Consumer") and [export](/wiki/Export "Export") sectors of the economy, the creation of an urban [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class") that now constitutes 15% of the population, higher living standards (which is shown via dramatic increases in [GDP per capita](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product"), [consumer spending](/wiki/Consumer_spending "Consumer spending"), [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy"), [literacy rate](/wiki/Literacy_rate "Literacy rate"), and total grain output) and a much wider range of personal rights and freedoms for average Chinese as evidence of the success of the reforms. Critics of the economic reforms, both in China and abroad, claim that the reforms have caused [wealth disparity](/wiki/Wealth_disparity "Wealth disparity"), [environmental pollution](/wiki/Environmental_pollution "Environmental pollution"), rampant [corruption](/wiki/Political_corruption "Political corruption"), widespread [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment") and [layoffs](/wiki/Layoff "Layoff") at inefficient [state-owned enterprises](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise "State-owned enterprise") under the state policy of [Xiagang](/wiki/Xiagang "Xiagang") (下岗), and has introduced often unwelcome cultural influences. Consequently, they believe that China's culture has been corrupted, the poor have been reduced to a hopeless abject underclass, and that the social stability is threatened. They are also of the opinion that various political reforms, such as moves towards popular elections, have been unfairly nipped in the bud. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After all, the path of modernisation and market-oriented economic reforms that China started since the early 1980s appears to be fundamentally unchallenged. Even critics of China's market reforms do not wish to see a backtrack of these two decades of reforms, but rather propose corrective measures to offset some of the social issues caused by existing reforms. On the other hand, in 1979, the Chinese government instituted a [one child policy](/wiki/One_child_policy "One child policy") to try to control its rapidly increasing population. The controversial policy resulted in a dramatic decrease in child poverty. The law was eliminated in 2015.[[130]](#cite_note-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131)

### Political reforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Political reforms")]

See also: [1982 Constitution of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/1982_Constitution_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "1982 Constitution of the People's Republic of China"), [Socialism with Chinese characteristics](/wiki/Socialism_with_Chinese_characteristics "Socialism with Chinese characteristics"), and [Primary stage of socialism](/wiki/Primary_stage_of_socialism "Primary stage of socialism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Zhao_Ziyang_%281985%29.jpg/250px-Zhao_Ziyang_%281985%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zhao_Ziyang_(1985).jpg)

[Zhao Ziyang](/wiki/Zhao_Ziyang "Zhao Ziyang"), a leading reformist, was assigned by Deng to take charge of the political reforms since 1986. However, he was forced to leave his position as the [General Secretary of CCP](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") after the [1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests_and_massacre "1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre"), while the majority of the planned political reforms (after 1986) ended drastically.

On 18 August 1980, Deng Xiaoping gave a speech titled "On the Reform of the Party and State Leadership System" (党和国家领导制度改革) at an enlarged meeting of the [Political Bureau of the CCP Central Committee](/wiki/Politburo_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party") in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), launching the political reforms in China.[[132]](#cite_note-Deng-1980-132)[[133]](#cite_note-China_Daily-2011-133)[[134]](#cite_note-Ng-Quinn-1982-134)[[135]](#cite_note-Whyte-1993-135) He called for the end of bureaucracy, centralisation of power as well as patriarchy, proposing term limits to the leading positions in China and advocating the "[democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism")" as well as the "[collective leadership](/wiki/Collective_leadership "Collective leadership")".[[133]](#cite_note-China_Daily-2011-133)[[134]](#cite_note-Ng-Quinn-1982-134)[[135]](#cite_note-Whyte-1993-135) In addition, Deng proposed to the [National People's Congress](/wiki/National_People%27s_Congress "National People's Congress") a systematic revision of China's constitution (the [1978 Constitution](/wiki/1978_Constitution_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "1978 Constitution of the People's Republic of China")), and emphasized that the Constitution must be able to protect the [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights") of Chinese citizens and must reflect the principle of [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers"); he also described the idea of "[collective leadership](/wiki/Collective_leadership "Collective leadership")" and championed the principle of "[one man, one vote](/wiki/One_man,_one_vote "One man, one vote")" among leaders to avoid the dictatorship of the [General Secretary of CCP](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_CPC_Central_Committee "General Secretary of CPC Central Committee").[[132]](#cite_note-Deng-1980-132)[[135]](#cite_note-Whyte-1993-135)[[136]](#cite_note-CLG-1987-136) In December 1982, the fourth Constitution of the People's Republic, known as the "[1982 Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Constitution of the People's Republic of China")", was passed by the [5th National People's Congress](/wiki/5th_National_People%27s_Congress "5th National People's Congress"), embodying Chinese-style [constitutionalism](/wiki/Constitutionalism "Constitutionalism") with most of its content still being effective as of today.[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138)

In the first half of 1986, Deng repeatedly called for the revival of political reforms, as further economic reforms were hindered by the original political system while the country had seen an increasing trend of [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_China "Corruption in China") and [economic inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality"), aggravated by the many [social privileges](/wiki/Social_privilege "Social privilege") enjoyed by governmental officials and their relatives.[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140) A five-man research unit for China's political reforms was established in September 1986, and the members included [Zhao Ziyang](/wiki/Zhao_Ziyang "Zhao Ziyang"), [Hu Qili](/wiki/Hu_Qili "Hu Qili"), [Tian Jiyun](/wiki/Tian_Jiyun "Tian Jiyun"), [Bo Yibo](/wiki/Bo_Yibo "Bo Yibo") and [Peng Chong](/wiki/Peng_Chong "Peng Chong").[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-142) Deng's intention of political reforms was to boost the administrative efficiency, further separate the responsibilities between the Communist Party and the Government, and to eliminate bureaucracy.[[143]](#cite_note-Wu-2014-143)[[144]](#cite_note-Wu-2014-1-144) Although he also mentioned "[rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law")" and "[democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy")", Deng delimited the reforms within the [one-party system](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state") and opposed the implementation of [Western-style](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") constitutionalism.[[144]](#cite_note-Wu-2014-1-144)[[145]](#cite_note-Lim-2019-145) In October 1987, at the [13th National Congress of CCP](/wiki/13th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "13th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") chaired by Deng, [Zhao Ziyang](/wiki/Zhao_Ziyang "Zhao Ziyang") delivered an important talk drafted by [Bao Tong](/wiki/Bao_Tong "Bao Tong") on the political reforms.[[146]](#cite_note-Schram-1988-146)[[147]](#cite_note-Dirlik-2019-147) In his speech titled "Advance Along the Road of [Socialism with Chinese characteristics](/wiki/Socialism_with_Chinese_characteristics "Socialism with Chinese characteristics")" (沿着有中国特色的社会主义道路前进), Zhao argued that the socialism in China was still in its [primary stage](/wiki/Primary_stage_of_socialism "Primary stage of socialism") and by taking Deng's speech in 1980 as guidelines, Zhao outlined a variety of steps to be taken for the political reforms, including promoting the rule of law and the [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers"), imposing [de-centralisation](/wiki/Decentralization "Decentralization"), and improving the [election system](/wiki/Electoral_system "Electoral system").[[143]](#cite_note-Wu-2014-143)[[146]](#cite_note-Schram-1988-146)[[147]](#cite_note-Dirlik-2019-147) At this Congress, Zhao was elected as the new General Secretary of CCP.[[148]](#cite_note-148)

However, after the [1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests_and_massacre "1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre"), many leading reformists including Zhao and Bao were removed from their posts, and the majority of the planned political reforms (after 1986) ended drastically.[[145]](#cite_note-Lim-2019-145)[[149]](#cite_note-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150) Left-wing conservatives led by [Chen Yun](/wiki/Chen_Yun "Chen Yun"), President [Li Xiannian](/wiki/Li_Xiannian "Li Xiannian") and Premier [Li Peng](/wiki/Li_Peng "Li Peng") took control until [Deng Xiaoping's southern tour](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping%27s_southern_tour "Deng Xiaoping's southern tour") in early 1992. On the other hand, many policies due to the political reforms launched by Deng in the early 1980s remain effective after 1989 (such as the new Constitution, term limits, and the democratic centralism), even though some of them have been reversed by CCP general secretary [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") after 2012.[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153)

### Political turmoil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Political turmoil")]

Main articles: [1986 Chinese student demonstrations](/wiki/1986_Chinese_student_demonstrations "1986 Chinese student demonstrations") and [1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests_and_massacre "1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Ud%C3%A1losti_na_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Tian_an_men%2C_%C4%8C%C3%ADna_1989%2C_foto_Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Tondl.jpg/250px-Ud%C3%A1losti_na_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Tian_an_men%2C_%C4%8C%C3%ADna_1989%2C_foto_Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Tondl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ud%C3%A1losti_na_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Tian_an_men,_%C4%8C%C3%ADna_1989,_foto_Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Tondl.jpg)

The [1989 Tiananmen Square protests](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests "1989 Tiananmen Square protests"), which was ended by a military-led massacre

In 1983, left-wing conservatives initiated the "[Anti-Spiritual Pollution Campaign](/wiki/Anti-Spiritual_Pollution_Campaign "Anti-Spiritual Pollution Campaign")".[[154]](#cite_note-:1-154)

In 1986, the [student demonstrations](/wiki/1986_Chinese_student_demonstrations "1986 Chinese student demonstrations") led to the resignation of [Hu Yaobang](/wiki/Hu_Yaobang "Hu Yaobang"), then General Secretary of CCP and a leading reformist, and the left-wing conservatives continued to launch the "[Anti-Bourgeois Liberalisation Campaign](/wiki/Bourgeois_liberalization "Bourgeois liberalization")".[[155]](#cite_note-:2-155) The campaign ended in mid-1987 because [Zhao Ziyang](/wiki/Zhao_Ziyang "Zhao Ziyang") convinced [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") that the conservatives were taking advantage of the campaign to oppose the [Reforms and Opening-up](/wiki/Reforms_and_Opening_Up "Reforms and Opening Up") program.[[155]](#cite_note-:2-155)

Although standards of living improved significantly in the 1980s, Deng's reforms were not without criticism. Hard-liners asserted that Deng opened China once again to various social evils, and an overall increase in materialistic thinking, while liberals attacked Deng's unrelenting stance on *wider* political reforms. Liberal forces began gathering in different forms to protest against the Party's authoritarian leadership. In 1989, the death of Hu Yaobang, a liberal figure, triggered weeks of spontaneous protests in the [Tiananmen Square](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests "1989 Tiananmen Square protests"). The government imposed martial law and sent in tanks and soldiers to suppress the demonstrations. [Western countries](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") and multilateral organisations briefly suspended their formal ties with China's government under Premier [Li Peng](/wiki/Li_Peng "Li Peng")'s leadership, which was directly responsible for the military curfew and bloody crackdown.[[156]](#cite_note-156)

### Military modernisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Military modernisation")]

Main articles: [Modernization of the People's Liberation Army](/wiki/Modernization_of_the_People%27s_Liberation_Army "Modernization of the People's Liberation Army") and [History of the People's Liberation Army](/wiki/History_of_the_People%27s_Liberation_Army "History of the People's Liberation Army")

In early 1979, China started a [one-month war with Vietnam](/wiki/Sino-Vietnamese_War "Sino-Vietnamese War"). Furthermore, China continued to support [Khmer Rouge](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge "Khmer Rouge") [during Deng Xiaoping's time](/wiki/Cambodian_genocide#Deng_era "Cambodian genocide") together with the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") and several other countries to counter the regional influence of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").[[157]](#cite_note-157)

In March 1981, Deng Xiaoping determined that a [military exercise](/wiki/Military_exercise "Military exercise") was necessary for the [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") (PLA), and in September 1981, the [North China Military Exercise](/wiki/North_China_Military_Exercise "North China Military Exercise") took place, becoming the largest exercise conducted by the PLA since the founding of the People's Republic. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1985, in order to modernise the PLA and to save money, Deng cut 1 million troops from the military (百万大裁军) and ordered further modernisation.[[158]](#cite_note-LATimes-1985-158)

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

See also: [Deng Xiaoping's 1979 visit to the United States](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping%27s_1979_visit_to_the_United_States "Deng Xiaoping's 1979 visit to the United States"), [Sino-British Joint Declaration](/wiki/Sino-British_Joint_Declaration "Sino-British Joint Declaration"), and [1989 Sino-Soviet Summit](/wiki/1989_Sino-Soviet_Summit "1989 Sino-Soviet Summit")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Carter_DengXiaoping.jpg/250px-Carter_DengXiaoping.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carter_DengXiaoping.jpg)

[Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") and [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") in 1979

On 1 January 1979, the People's Republic of China formally [established its diplomatic relations with the United States](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_relations "China–United States relations").[[159]](#cite_note-159) In January 1979, Deng Xiaoping visited the United States, which was first official visit by a [paramount leader of China](/wiki/Paramount_leader "Paramount leader") to the United States.[[160]](#cite_note-160) In the same year, the [Chinese Olympic Committee](/wiki/Chinese_Olympic_Committee "Chinese Olympic Committee") for PRC was recognized by the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee"). Under the advice of [Lee Kuan Yew](/wiki/Lee_Kuan_Yew "Lee Kuan Yew"), Deng Xiaoping agreed to further open up the country and stop exporting communist ideologies and revolutions to other countries like Mao did, and the decisions significantly improved the relations between China and many countries, especially those in [south-east Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia").[[161]](#cite_note-161)[[162]](#cite_note-162)

In 1984, [Xu Haifeng](/wiki/Xu_Haifeng "Xu Haifeng"), a pistol [shooter](/wiki/Shooting_sports "Shooting sports"), won the first Olympic gold medal for China during the [1984 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). In the same year, the *[Sino-British Joint Declaration](/wiki/Sino-British_Joint_Declaration "Sino-British Joint Declaration")* was signed by [China](/wiki/China "China") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), stipulating that the sovereignty and the administrative management of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") would be handed over back to China on 1 July 1997 under the "[one country, two systems](/wiki/One_country,_two_systems "One country, two systems")" framework. In 1987, the *[Joint Declaration on the Question of Macau](/wiki/Joint_Declaration_on_the_Question_of_Macau "Joint Declaration on the Question of Macau")* was signed by China and Portugal, stipulating that the sovereignty and the administrative management of [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") would be handed over back to China 20 December 1999, again under the "one country, two systems" framework. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1989, the relation between China and the Soviet Union returned to normal for the first time since the [Sino-Soviet split](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split "Sino-Soviet split") in the 1950s. [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev"), then [General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union"), visited [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") and met with Deng Xiaoping during the [Sino-Soviet Summit](/wiki/1989_Sino-Soviet_Summit "1989 Sino-Soviet Summit"), which took place amid the Tiananmen Square protests.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

After the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre, China faced strong backlash from the [western countries](/wiki/Western_Countries "Western Countries").[[164]](#cite_note-164) Deng, as a response, devised a new set of diplomatic strategies for China, which were summarised to be "hide your strength, bide your time, never take the lead".[[165]](#cite_note-Harshaw-2020-165)[[166]](#cite_note-Heydarian-2020-166) In the 1980s and early 1990s, People's Republic of China continued to establish formal diplomatic relations with a number of countries such as [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") (1984), [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar") (1988), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") (1990), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") (1990), [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") (1992) and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") (1992).[[81]](#cite_note-MFA-2020-81)
