### Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Controversies")]

See also: [Boluan Fanzheng § Controversies](/wiki/Boluan_Fanzheng#Controversies "Boluan Fanzheng"), and [Chinese Democracy Movement](/wiki/Chinese_Democracy_Movement "Chinese Democracy Movement")

After the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"), Deng started the *Boluan Fanzheng* program to correct the [Maoist](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism") mistakes, but some of his policies and views were controversial. Deng insisted on praising that Mao had done "7 good and 3 bad" for the Chinese people, while attributing numerous disasters in the Cultural Revolution to [Lin Biao](/wiki/Lin_Biao "Lin Biao") and the [Gang of Four](/wiki/Gang_of_Four "Gang of Four").[[174]](#cite_note-174) In addition, in order to maintain the [one-party state](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state") in China for the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP), Deng proposed the "[Four Cardinal Principles](/wiki/Four_Cardinal_Principles "Four Cardinal Principles")" at the [CCP Theory Conference](/wiki/Theory_Conference,_January-April_1979 "Theory Conference, January-April 1979") in March 1979, which soon became the limit of [political liberalization](/wiki/Political_liberalization "Political liberalization") in mainland China and were incorporated in the [Constitution of China](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Constitution of the People's Republic of China") (1982).[[175]](#cite_note-175)

Moreover, the role that Deng played in the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre was rather controversial.[[176]](#cite_note-176)[[177]](#cite_note-177) In fact, he also cracked down the [Democracy Wall movement](/wiki/Democracy_Wall "Democracy Wall") as well as the [Beijing Spring](/wiki/Beijing_Spring "Beijing Spring") in early 1980s.[[178]](#cite_note-178) Political campaigns such as [Campaign against spiritual pollution](/wiki/Campaign_against_spiritual_pollution "Campaign against spiritual pollution") in 1983 and [Anti-Bourgeois Liberalisation Campaign](/wiki/Bourgeois_liberalization "Bourgeois liberalization") in 1986-1987 were also launched, and many [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism_in_China "Liberalism in China") officials and intellectuals including [Hu Yaobang](/wiki/Hu_Yaobang "Hu Yaobang") were purged or penalized.[[154]](#cite_note-:1-154)[[155]](#cite_note-:2-155)

To cope with the population crisis after Mao's era, Deng Xiaoping, together with other senior officials including [Chen Yun](/wiki/Chen_Yun "Chen Yun") and [Li Xiannian](/wiki/Li_Xiannian "Li Xiannian"), supported the implementation of the "[one-child policy](/wiki/One-child_policy "One-child policy")".[[179]](#cite_note-179) Some of the extreme measures in practice created many controversies such as human rights violations.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

## Jiang Zemin and the third generation (1989–2002)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Jiang Zemin and the third generation (1989–2002)")]

See also: [History of the People's Republic of China (1989–2002)](/wiki/History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China_(1989%E2%80%932002) "History of the People's Republic of China (1989–2002)")

### Transition of power and Deng's Southern Tour

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Transition of power and Deng's Southern Tour")]

Main article: [Deng Xiaoping's southern tour](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping%27s_southern_tour "Deng Xiaoping's southern tour")

After the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre, Deng Xiaoping stepped away from public view and fully retired. Power passed to the third generation of leadership led by [Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin"), who was hailed as its "core". However, owing to the Tiananmen massacre, the [Reforms and Opening-up program](/wiki/Reforms_and_Opening_Up "Reforms and Opening Up") went into stagnation in early 1990s, and Jiang, supported by left-wing conservatives, was not doing enough to continue the reforms. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the spring of 1992, Deng made his [famous tour](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping%27s_Southern_Tour "Deng Xiaoping's Southern Tour") to [southern China](/wiki/Southern_China "Southern China"), which is widely regarded as a critical point in the history of modern China as it saved China's economic reform as well as the [capital market](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market") ([Shanghai Stock Exchange](/wiki/Shanghai_Stock_Exchange "Shanghai Stock Exchange") and [Shenzhen Stock Exchange](/wiki/Shenzhen_Stock_Exchange "Shenzhen Stock Exchange")), and preserved the stability of the society. Jiang eventually sided with Deng and publicly supported the Reforms and Opening-up program. Conservative [Li Peng](/wiki/Li_Peng "Li Peng") was the [Premier of China](/wiki/Premier_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Premier of the People's Republic of China") until 1998, when reformist [Zhu Rongji](/wiki/Zhu_Rongji "Zhu Rongji") succeeded as the new Premier.[[181]](#cite_note-181)

### Domestic affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Domestic affairs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Jiang_Zemin_St._Petersburg_%28restored%29.jpg/250px-Jiang_Zemin_St._Petersburg_%28restored%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jiang_Zemin_St._Petersburg_(restored).jpg)

[Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin")

Economic growth achieved a sustained high rate by the mid-1990s. Jiang Zemin's macroeconomic reforms furthered Deng's vision for "[Socialism with Chinese characteristics](/wiki/Socialism_with_Chinese_characteristics "Socialism with Chinese characteristics")". Jiang laid heavy emphasis on scientific and technological advancement in areas such as [space exploration](/wiki/Space_exploration "Space exploration"). At the same time, Jiang's period saw a continued rise in social corruption in all areas of life. [Unemployment](/wiki/Xiagang "Xiagang") skyrocketed as unprofitable [State-owned enterprises](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise "State-owned enterprise") (SOE) were closed to make way for more competitive ventures internally and abroad. The ill-equipped social welfare system was put on a serious test.[[182]](#cite_note-182) In 2000, Jiang proposed his ideology of "[Three Represents](/wiki/Three_Represents "Three Represents")", which was ratified by the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") at the [Sixteenth Party Congress](/wiki/16th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "16th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") in 2002. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At the same time, Premier [Zhu Rongji](/wiki/Zhu_Rongji "Zhu Rongji")'s economic policies held China's economy strong during the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis"). Economic growth averaged at 8% annually, pushed back by the [1998 Yangtze River Floods](/wiki/1998_China_floods "1998 China floods"). Standards of living improved significantly, although a wide urban-rural [wealth gap](/wiki/Wealth_gap "Wealth gap") was created as China saw the reappearance of the [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class"). Wealth disparity between the Eastern coastal regions and the Western hinterlands continued to widen by the day, prompting government programs to "[develop the West](/wiki/China_Western_Development "China Western Development")", taking on ambitious projects such as the [Qinghai–Tibet railway](/wiki/Qinghai%E2%80%93Tibet_railway "Qinghai–Tibet railway"). However, rampant corruption continued despite Premier Zhu's anti-corruption campaign that executed many officials. Corruption alone was estimated to amount to the equivalent of anywhere from 10 to 20 per cent of China's GDP.[[183]](#cite_note-183)

To sustain the increased electricity consumption, the [Three Gorges Dam](/wiki/Three_Gorges_Dam "Three Gorges Dam") was built, attracting supporters and widespread criticism. Environmental pollution became a very serious problem as Beijing was frequently hit by [sandstorms](/wiki/Dust_storm "Dust storm") as a result of [desertification](/wiki/Desertification "Desertification").[[184]](#cite_note-184)

In 1990s, [Project 211](/wiki/Project_211 "Project 211") and [Project 985](/wiki/Project_985 "Project 985") were launched for [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education") in China. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Doha_Ministerial_Conference_9-13_November_2001_%289308713368%29.jpg/250px-Doha_Ministerial_Conference_9-13_November_2001_%289308713368%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Doha_Ministerial_Conference_9-13_November_2001_(9308713368).jpg)

On 10 November 2001, the Ministerial Conference of the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (WTO) in [Doha](/wiki/Doha "Doha") approved China's entry into the WTO.[[185]](#cite_note-185)

In November 1991, China joined the [Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation](/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation "Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation"). The 1990s saw the peaceful [Handover of Hong Kong](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") by the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") respectively to China.[[186]](#cite_note-186) Hong Kong and Macau mostly continued their own governance, retaining independence in their economic, social, and judicial systems until 2019, when [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") tried to expand national powers in the face of [large-scale protests in Hong Kong](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Hong_Kong_protests "2019–20 Hong Kong protests").[[187]](#cite_note-187)[[188]](#cite_note-188)

[Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin") and [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") exchanged state visits, but [Sino-American relations](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_relations "China–United States relations") took very sour turns at the end of the decade, especially after the [third Taiwan Strait Crisis](/wiki/Third_Taiwan_Strait_Crisis "Third Taiwan Strait Crisis"). On 7 May 1999, during the [Kosovo War](/wiki/Kosovo_War "Kosovo War"), [U.S. aircraft bombed the Chinese embassy in Belgrade](/wiki/United_States_bombing_of_the_Chinese_embassy_in_Belgrade "United States bombing of the Chinese embassy in Belgrade"). The U.S. government claimed the strike was due to bad intelligence and false target identification.[[189]](#cite_note-189) Inside the United States, the [Cox Report](/wiki/Cox_Report "Cox Report") stated that China had been stealing various top United States military secrets.[[190]](#cite_note-190) In 2001, a United States surveillance plane [collided](/wiki/Hainan_Island_incident "Hainan Island incident") with a Chinese fighter jet over international waters near [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan"), inciting further outrage with the Chinese public, already dissatisfied with the United States.[[191]](#cite_note-191)

After a decade of talks, China was finally admitted into the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") in 2001. The same year saw the establishment of the [Shanghai Cooperation Organisation](/wiki/Shanghai_Cooperation_Organisation "Shanghai Cooperation Organisation").[[192]](#cite_note-192) In August 2002, due to the efforts of the renowned mathematician [Shiing-Shen Chern](/wiki/Shiing-Shen_Chern "Shiing-Shen Chern"), the quadrennial [International Congress of Mathematicians](/wiki/International_Congress_of_Mathematicians "International Congress of Mathematicians") was held in Beijing – the first time in a [developing country](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country"), with Chern being the honorary president of the Congress and [Wu Wenjun](/wiki/Wu_Wenjun "Wu Wenjun") being the president.[[193]](#cite_note-193)

### Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Controversies")]

On the political agenda, China was once again put on the spotlight for the banning of public [Falun Gong](/wiki/Falun_Gong "Falun Gong") activity in 1999. Silent protesters from the spiritual movement sat outside of [Zhongnanhai](/wiki/Zhongnanhai "Zhongnanhai"), asking for dialogue with China's leaders. Jiang saw it as a threat to the political situation and outlawed the group altogether, while using mass media propaganda[[201]](#cite_note-201) to denounce it as an "evil cult".[[202]](#cite_note-202)

[Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin"), after formally retiring as the [paramount leader of China](/wiki/Paramount_Leader_of_China "Paramount Leader of China") in 2004, was believed to have moved behind the scenes and was still in control of the country even after his late step-down from the [Chairman of the Central Military Commission](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Chairman of the Central Military Commission (China)") in 2005.[[203]](#cite_note-203)[[204]](#cite_note-Huang-2012-204)[[205]](#cite_note-Wong-2012-205) The [Jiang faction](/wiki/Shanghai_clique "Shanghai clique"), including [Zhou Yongkang](/wiki/Zhou_Yongkang "Zhou Yongkang"), [Guo Boxiong](/wiki/Guo_Boxiong "Guo Boxiong") and [Xu Caihou](/wiki/Xu_Caihou "Xu Caihou"), continued to impact China significantly after [Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao") succeeded as the paramount leader of China.[[204]](#cite_note-Huang-2012-204)[[205]](#cite_note-Wong-2012-205)

## Hu Jintao and the fourth generation (2002–2012)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Hu Jintao and the fourth generation (2002–2012)")]

See also: [History of the People's Republic of China (2002–present)](/wiki/History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China_(2002%E2%80%93present) "History of the People's Republic of China (2002–present)")

### Transition of power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Transition of power")]

Main article: [Hu–Wen Administration](/wiki/Hu%E2%80%93Wen_Administration "Hu–Wen Administration")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Hu_Jintao_Cannes2011.jpg/250px-Hu_Jintao_Cannes2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hu_Jintao_Cannes2011.jpg)

[Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao")

[Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao") succeeded as the [General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") in November 2002.[[206]](#cite_note-206) In March 2003, Hu Jintao became the 6th [President of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/President_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "President of the People's Republic of China"), with [Wen Jiabao](/wiki/Wen_Jiabao "Wen Jiabao") being the [Premier of China](/wiki/Premier_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Premier of the People's Republic of China"). In September 2004, Hu Jintao became the [Chairman of the Central Military Commission](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Chairman of the Central Military Commission (China)"). [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Domestic affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Domestic affairs")]

The economy continued to grow in double-digit numbers as the development of rural areas became the major focus of government policy. In 2010, China overtook Japan as the [world's second-largest economy](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)").[[207]](#cite_note-207)[[208]](#cite_note-208) The assertion of the [Scientific Perspective](/wiki/Scientific_Outlook_on_Development "Scientific Outlook on Development") to create a [Socialist Harmonious Society](/wiki/Harmonious_Society "Harmonious Society") was the focus of the Hu Jintao – Wen Jiabao administration, as some Jiang Zemin-era excesses were slowly reversed. In late 2002, the [South–North Water Transfer Project](/wiki/South%E2%80%93North_Water_Transfer_Project "South–North Water Transfer Project") began construction. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In gradual steps to consolidate his power, [Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao") removed Shanghai Party secretary [Chen Liangyu](/wiki/Chen_Liangyu "Chen Liangyu") and other potential political opponents amidst the fight against corruption, and the ongoing struggle against once powerful [Shanghai clique](/wiki/Shanghai_clique "Shanghai clique"). In particular, in 2012, the [Wang Lijun incident](/wiki/Wang_Lijun_incident "Wang Lijun incident") and the scandal of [Bo Xilai](/wiki/Bo_Xilai "Bo Xilai") received widespread attention and media coverage.[[209]](#cite_note-209)[[210]](#cite_note-210)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/2008_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony_-_Fireworks.jpg/250px-2008_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony_-_Fireworks.jpg)](/wiki/File:2008_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony_-_Fireworks.jpg)

The [2008 Beijing Olympics](/wiki/2008_Beijing_Olympics "2008 Beijing Olympics")

The continued economic growth of the country as well as its sporting power status gained China the right to host the [2008 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics"). However, this also put Hu Jintao's administration under intense spotlight. While the 2008 Olympics was commonly understood to be a come-out party for People's Republic of China, in light of the [March 2008 Tibet protests](/wiki/2008_Tibetan_unrest "2008 Tibetan unrest"), the government received heavy scrutiny. The [Olympic torch](/wiki/Olympic_torch "Olympic torch") was met with protest en route. Within the country, these reactions were met with a fervent wave of [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") with accusations of [Western](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") bias against [China](/wiki/China "China").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Continued economic growth during the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") which started in the United States and hobbled the world economy increased China's confidence in its model of development and convinced elites that the global balance of power was shifting.[[211]](#cite_note-211) In the Chinese view, the cause of the crisis was Western countries' "inappropriate macroeconomic policies" and "unsustainable modes of development."[[212]](#cite_note-212) When Western countries were nearing financial disaster, China created credit for spending on infrastructure.[[213]](#cite_note-Mitter-2020-213) This both helped stabilize the global economy and it also provided an opportunity for China to retool its own infrastructure.[[213]](#cite_note-Mitter-2020-213) China increased its standing as a responsible global actor during the crisis.[[213]](#cite_note-Mitter-2020-213)

Meanwhile, a number of scientific progresses and breakthroughs took place between 2002 and 2012, many of which originated from the [863 Program](/wiki/863_Program "863 Program"). In 2003, China successfully sent an [astronaut](/wiki/Astronaut "Astronaut"), [Yang Liwei](/wiki/Yang_Liwei "Yang Liwei"), to the space via [Shenzhou 5](/wiki/Shenzhou_5 "Shenzhou 5"), becoming the third country in the world to do so independently after the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").[[214]](#cite_note-214) In 2010, *[Jiaolong](/wiki/Jiaolong_(submersible) "Jiaolong (submersible)"),* the Chinese manned deep-sea research [submersible](/wiki/Submersible "Submersible"), was deployed. In 2011–2012, [BeiDou-2](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou"), the Chinese [satellite navigation system](/wiki/Satellite_navigation_system "Satellite navigation system"), became operational. In 2011, [Tiangong-1](/wiki/Tiangong-1 "Tiangong-1"), the first prototype [space station](/wiki/Space_station "Space station") of China, was successfully launched.[[215]](#cite_note-215) In March 2012, results from the [Daya Bay Reactor Neutrino Experiment](/wiki/Daya_Bay_Reactor_Neutrino_Experiment "Daya Bay Reactor Neutrino Experiment") in [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") received international attention.[[216]](#cite_note-216) In October 2012, [Mo Yan](/wiki/Mo_Yan "Mo Yan") became the first Chinese citizen (mainland) to win the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature").[[217]](#cite_note-217)

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

China's position in the [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror") drew the country closer diplomatically to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). In 2010, the [Asian Games](/wiki/2010_Asian_Games "2010 Asian Games") was held in [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou"), and in 2011, the [Summer Universiade](/wiki/2011_Summer_Universiade "2011 Summer Universiade") was held in [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen"). In 2010, another international event took place in China— [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") held the [World Expo](/wiki/Expo_2010 "Expo 2010") for the first time. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [political status](/wiki/Political_status_of_Taiwan "Political status of Taiwan") and future of [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") remain uncertain, but steps have been taken to improving relations between the Communist Party and several of Taiwan's parties that hold a less antagonistic view towards China, notably former rival [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang"). [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Hu's critics say that his government was overly aggressive in asserting its new power, overestimated its reach, and raised the ire and apprehension of various neighbours, including Southeast Asian countries, India, and Japan. Such policies are also said to be provocative towards the United States.[[218]](#cite_note-econo-218)

### Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Controversies")]

In the years after Hu Jintao's rise to power, respect of basic [human rights in China](/wiki/Human_rights_in_China "Human rights in China") continued to be a source of concern. [Liu Xiaobo](/wiki/Liu_Xiaobo "Liu Xiaobo"), [Nobel Peace Prize winner](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_Peace_Prize_laureates "List of Nobel Peace Prize laureates") and [human rights activist](/wiki/Human_rights_defender "Human rights defender"), was arrested and sentenced to jail for 11 years in 2010.[[223]](#cite_note-Brown-2010-223)[[224]](#cite_note-Factbox-2010-224) Liu Xiaobo, together with others, authored the [Charter 08](/wiki/Charter_08 "Charter 08") and received the Nobel Peace Prize in 2010.[[223]](#cite_note-Brown-2010-223)[[224]](#cite_note-Factbox-2010-224) Liu Xiaobo died in 2017.

In Hu Jintao's time, the Chinese Communist Party and the Chinese government created the "[50 Cent Party](/wiki/50_Cent_Party "50 Cent Party")", attempting to "guide" public opinions online in favor of the Communist Party and the Chinese government.[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[226]](#cite_note-226)

## Xi Jinping and the fifth generation (2012–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Xi Jinping and the fifth generation (2012–present)")]

See also: [History of the People's Republic of China (2002–present)](/wiki/History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China_(2002%E2%80%93present) "History of the People's Republic of China (2002–present)")

### Transition of power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Transition of power")]

See also: [Xi–Li Administration](/wiki/Xi%E2%80%93Li_Administration "Xi–Li Administration") and [General secretaryship of Xi Jinping](/wiki/General_secretaryship_of_Xi_Jinping "General secretaryship of Xi Jinping")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Xi_Jinping_2019.jpg/250px-Xi_Jinping_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xi_Jinping_2019.jpg)

[General Secretary](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping")

[Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") became the [General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") and the [Chairman of the Central Military Commission](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Chairman of the Central Military Commission (China)"), the two most powerful positions on 15 November 2012.[[227]](#cite_note-227) And on March 14, 2013, he became the 7th [President of China](/wiki/President_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "President of the People's Republic of China").[[228]](#cite_note-228) [Li Keqiang](/wiki/Li_Keqiang "Li Keqiang") became the [Premier of China](/wiki/Premier_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Premier of the People's Republic of China") in March 2013. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In October 2022, [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") was [re-elected](/wiki/20th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "20th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") as [General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") for a precedent-breaking third term of [paramount leader](/wiki/Paramount_leader "Paramount leader") after [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong")'s death.[[229]](#cite_note-229) In March 2023, Xi continued as the President of China for a third term, while [Li Qiang](/wiki/Li_Qiang "Li Qiang") succeeded Li Keqiang as the new Premier of China. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Domestic affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Domestic affairs")]

See also: [Xi Jinping Thought](/wiki/Xi_Jinping_Thought "Xi Jinping Thought")

A massive, long-term [anti-corruption campaign](/wiki/Anti-corruption_campaign_under_Xi_Jinping "Anti-corruption campaign under Xi Jinping") has been carried out under Xi Jinping since 2012, mostly targeting Xi Jinping's political rivals such as members of the [Jiang faction](/wiki/Shanghai_clique "Shanghai clique") including Party senior leaders [Zhou Yongkang](/wiki/Zhou_Yongkang "Zhou Yongkang"), [Guo Boxiong](/wiki/Guo_Boxiong "Guo Boxiong") and [Xu Caihou](/wiki/Xu_Caihou "Xu Caihou").[[230]](#cite_note-230)[[231]](#cite_note-231)

In March 2018, the Party-controlled [National People's Congress](/wiki/National_People%27s_Congress "National People's Congress") passed a set of [constitutional amendments](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Constitution of the People's Republic of China") including the removal of [term limits](/wiki/Term_limits "Term limits") for the [president](/wiki/President_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "President of the People's Republic of China") and [vice president](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Vice President of the People's Republic of China"), the creation of a [National Supervisory Commission](/wiki/National_Supervisory_Commission "National Supervisory Commission"), as well as enhancing the central role of the Communist Party.[[232]](#cite_note-232)[[233]](#cite_note-233) On 17 March 2018, the Chinese legislature re-appointed Xi Jinping as president, now [without term limits](/wiki/President_for_life "President for life").[[234]](#cite_note-234)[[235]](#cite_note-235) According to the *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")*, Xi Jinping expressed his views of constitutional amendment at meetings with Chinese officials and foreign dignitaries. Xi Jinping explained the decision in terms of needing to align two more powerful posts – [General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") and [Chairman of the Central Military Commission](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Chairman of the Central Military Commission (China)") (CMC) – which have no term limits. However, Xi Jinping did not say whether he intended to serve as party general secretary, CMC chairman and state president, for three or more terms.[[236]](#cite_note-236)

On the other hand, a series of scientific advances took place. In 2013, the [*Yutu* rover](/wiki/Yutu_(rover) "Yutu (rover)") was successfully deployed on the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") after the [Chang'e 3 lander](/wiki/Chang%27e_3 "Chang'e 3") landed on the Moon.[[237]](#cite_note-237) In 2015, [Tu Youyou](/wiki/Tu_Youyou "Tu Youyou") became the first Chinese citizen (mainland) to win the [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine").[[238]](#cite_note-238) In December 2015, the [Dark Matter Particle Explorer](/wiki/Dark_Matter_Particle_Explorer "Dark Matter Particle Explorer"), China's first [space observatory](/wiki/Space_telescope "Space telescope"), was successfully launched. The [Tiangong-2 space laboratory](/wiki/Tiangong-2 "Tiangong-2") was successfully launched in 2016, and in the same year the [Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical Telescope](/wiki/Five-hundred-meter_Aperture_Spherical_Telescope "Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical Telescope") (FAST) was built in [Guizhou](/wiki/Guizhou "Guizhou"). In 2018, the [Hong Kong–Zhuhai–Macau Bridge](/wiki/Hong_Kong%E2%80%93Zhuhai%E2%80%93Macau_Bridge "Hong Kong–Zhuhai–Macau Bridge"), world's longest sea-crossing bridge, was open to public.[[239]](#cite_note-239)

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

See also: [Foreign policy of Xi Jinping](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_Xi_Jinping "Foreign policy of Xi Jinping"), [Xi Jinping Thought on Diplomacy](/wiki/Xi_Jinping_Thought_on_Diplomacy "Xi Jinping Thought on Diplomacy"), and [Wolf warrior diplomacy](/wiki/Wolf_warrior_diplomacy "Wolf warrior diplomacy")

As Xi Jinping continued to consolidate power domestically, he gradually abandoned the diplomatic principles ("hide your strength, bide your time, never take the lead") set by [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") and appeared more as a "strongman" in the global stage.[[165]](#cite_note-Harshaw-2020-165)[[166]](#cite_note-Heydarian-2020-166)[[240]](#cite_note-240)[[241]](#cite_note-241) He launched the "[One Belt One Road initiative](/wiki/Belt_and_Road_Initiative "Belt and Road Initiative")" to make infrastructure investment in dozens of countries, which received widespread attention (both receptions and criticism) from around the world.[[242]](#cite_note-242)[[243]](#cite_note-243)

Since Xi Jinping succeeded as the leader of China, he tried to change "China's passivity" into an assertive strategy to defend China's claims over border and territory disputes such as [in the South China Sea](/wiki/Territorial_disputes_in_the_South_China_Sea "Territorial disputes in the South China Sea") and in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan").[[244]](#cite_note-244)[[245]](#cite_note-245) In 2018, [China–United States trade war](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_trade_war "China–United States trade war") started and significantly affected the global economy.[[246]](#cite_note-246)[[247]](#cite_note-247) In May 2020, [China–India skirmishes](/wiki/2020_China%E2%80%93India_skirmishes "2020 China–India skirmishes") along the border broke out and resulted in casualties.[[248]](#cite_note-248)

On the other hand, after Xi Jinping came to power, a number of international summits were held in China. In 2014, the [22nd annual gathering](/wiki/APEC_China_2014 "APEC China 2014") of [Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation](/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation "Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation") (APEC) leaders was held in Beijing; in 2016, [the G20 summit](/wiki/2016_G20_Hangzhou_summit "2016 G20 Hangzhou summit") was held in [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"); and in 2017, [the 9th BRICS summit](/wiki/9th_BRICS_summit "9th BRICS summit") was held in [Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"). Additionally, in 2015, the [Ma–Xi meeting](/wiki/First_Ma%E2%80%93Xi_meeting "First Ma–Xi meeting") in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") was the first meeting between the political leaders of the two sides of the [Taiwan Strait](/wiki/Taiwan_Strait "Taiwan Strait") since the end of the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") in 1950.

China refused to condemn the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), repeated Russian narratives about the war, opposed [economic sanctions against Russia](/wiki/International_sanctions_during_the_Russo-Ukrainian_War "International sanctions during the Russo-Ukrainian War"), and abstained or sided with Russia in UN votes on the war in Ukraine.[[249]](#cite_note-249)[[250]](#cite_note-250)

### Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Controversies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Hong_Kong_anti-extradition_bill_protest_%2848108527758%29.jpg/250px-Hong_Kong_anti-extradition_bill_protest_%2848108527758%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_anti-extradition_bill_protest_(48108527758).jpg)

The [2019 Hong Kong protests](/wiki/2019_Hong_Kong_Protests "2019 Hong Kong Protests")

Since 2012, Xi Jinping together with his allies has rolled back several policies from the [Boluan Fanzheng](/wiki/Boluan_Fanzheng "Boluan Fanzheng") period of [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") and promoted [his cult of personality](/wiki/Xi_Jinping%27s_cult_of_personality "Xi Jinping's cult of personality") as [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") did. For example, in 2018, Xi Jinping eliminated the term limit in China's Constitution for Chinese President, which challenged some of the political legacies of Deng Xiaoping and triggered concerns about a return to a one-man rule similar to Mao.[[254]](#cite_note-Hong-2016-254)[[255]](#cite_note-255)[[256]](#cite_note-256)[[257]](#cite_note-257)

After Xi Jinping came to power in 2012, the [Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_party "Chinese Communist party") along with the [Chinese government](/wiki/Government_of_China "Government of China") have significantly strengthened [their internet censorship](/wiki/Internet_censorship_in_China "Internet censorship in China") and tightened their control over the Chinese internet environment, blocking Chinese citizens' access to many foreign websites and mobile apps using the "[Great Firewall](/wiki/Great_Firewall "Great Firewall")".[[258]](#cite_note-258)[[259]](#cite_note-259)[[260]](#cite_note-260) At the same time, a large number of "[50 Cent Party](/wiki/50_Cent_Party "50 Cent Party")" members have been recruited to "guide" online narratives around the globe in favor of the Party and the Government.[[261]](#cite_note-261)[[262]](#cite_note-262) During the massive Hong Kong protests, for instance, [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") and [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") claimed to have removed or suspended over 200,000 accounts and pages linked with the Chinese government.[[263]](#cite_note-263)[[264]](#cite_note-264) As of 2022, the [mass surveillance system](/wiki/Mass_surveillance_in_China "Mass surveillance in China") keeps the whole population under close watch.[[30]](#cite_note-Jonathan_Fenby_2019-30)[[265]](#cite_note-265)

Globally, the aggressive "[wolf warrior diplomacy](/wiki/Wolf_warrior_diplomacy "Wolf warrior diplomacy")" under [general secretaryship of Xi Jinping](/wiki/General_secretaryship_of_Xi_Jinping "General secretaryship of Xi Jinping") has created numerous controversies and backlashes.[[266]](#cite_note-266)[[267]](#cite_note-267)[[268]](#cite_note-268) Controversies also surround China's handling of the outbreak of a [novel coronavirus](/wiki/Severe_acute_respiratory_syndrome_coronavirus_2 "Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2") ([COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19")) as well as its relationship with the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO) was rather controversial.[[269]](#cite_note-269)[[270]](#cite_note-270)[[271]](#cite_note-271) There have been a large number of [conspiracy theories and misinformation related to COVID-19](/wiki/Misinformation_related_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Misinformation related to the COVID-19 pandemic"), including the origin of the virus.[[272]](#cite_note-272) China has also launched its own disinformation campaign globally over the issues of the pandemic,and more, promoting China as a global leader while attacking the United States for instance.[[273]](#cite_note-273)[[274]](#cite_note-274)[[275]](#cite_note-275) Furthermore, manipulation of economic data by the Chinese government, such as publishing inflated GDP figures over the years, is also a major concern.[[276]](#cite_note-Leng-2019-276)[[277]](#cite_note-Zhang-2020-277)[[278]](#cite_note-278)
